text: For all inferences from experience suppose, as their foundation, that the future will resemble the past, and that similar powers will be conjoined with similar sensible qualities. If there be any suspicion that the course of nature may change, and that the past may be no rule for the future, all experience becomes useless, and can give rise to no inference or conclusion.
author: David Hume
source: Project Gutenberg eBook #9662 (An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding), plain text
out_pulls_note: Out-pulls the gloss: states the hidden premise ('the future will resemble the past') that the gloss only gestures at, exposing why habit rather than proof underwrites expectation.
search_notes: Gutenberg #9662, Section IV ('Sceptical Doubts'), Part II. Chose this clean no-italics passage over the equally famous 'That the sun will not rise to-morrow' line (Section IV, Part I), which carries italic underscores in the plain text and would need them preserved to byte-match. Verified verbatim substring after normalization. High confidence; canonical statement of the circularity at the heart of induction. No misattribution risk.
